---
title: "Independence and the i.i.d. Sample"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Probability Foundations"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

POINT  = "#2b3a67"     # data / samples
LINE   = "#c1121f"     # the curve / theoretical value
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights / the mean
GREY   = "#6c757d"
FILL   = "#9fb3d1"     # shaded areas
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This article is the immediate sequel to
> [PROB-07 *Joint Distributions, Covariance & Correlation*], which put two random variables
> in the same room: it built the **joint distribution** (their pmf/pdf taken together),
> recovered each one's **marginal** by summing or integrating the other out, and measured
> their tendency to move together with the **covariance**. [PROB-07] left one loose thread
> dangling on purpose — *zero covariance does not mean the variables are unrelated.* That
> thread is where this article begins. We define **independence** of random variables —
> the strongest possible statement that two quantities carry no information about each
> other — show how it factors the joint distribution into a product of marginals, prove
> that it *forces* covariance to zero (while the converse fails), and then collect the
> single most consequential assumption in all of applied statistics: that a dataset is
> **i.i.d.**, independent and identically distributed. Along the way we derive the rule
> that powers every limit theorem downstream — that **variances of independent variables
> add** — and watch the standard deviation of a sample mean shrink like $1/\sqrt{n}$. It
> assumes [PROB-07] (joint distributions, covariance), and beneath that [PROB-05]
> (expectation and its linearity) and [PROB-06] (variance). It sets up the i.i.d. backbone
> that [PROB-19 *Chebyshev's Inequality*] and [PROB-21 *Modes of Convergence*] need for the
> law of large numbers, that [PROB-22 *The Empirical CDF*] resamples from, and that the
> Statistical Inference, Regression, and Classification tracks open almost every analysis
> by assuming.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Two random variables are **independent** when learning the value of one tells you nothing
about the distribution of the other. This article makes that idea precise — independence
means the joint distribution **factors** into the product of the marginals,
$f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_X(x)\,f_Y(y)$ — and follows the factorization to its three most useful
consequences, every step on the page. First, **the expectation of a product factors**,
$\mathbb{E}[XY] = \mathbb{E}[X]\,\mathbb{E}[Y]$, from which **independence forces zero
covariance**; we then exhibit the famous counterexample showing the converse is false.
Second, while $\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_i X_i\right] = \sum_i \mathbb{E}[X_i]$ holds *always*
(linearity needs no independence), the variance of a sum collapses to a clean sum,
$\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\sum_i X_i\right) = \sum_i \operatorname{Var}(X_i)$, **only**
when the cross-covariances vanish — which independence guarantees. Third, applying this to
$n$ copies of one distribution gives the result the whole edifice rests on: the sample
mean has variance $\sigma^2/n$, so its standard deviation shrinks like $1/\sqrt{n}$. The
single claim the article defends is that **"i.i.d." is not a throwaway phrase but a precise,
load-bearing, and frequently violated assumption** — and that knowing exactly what it buys
you (and where real data breaks it) is the difference between using statistics and being
used by it.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Random variables are **independent** when their joint distribution factors into the
product of their marginals — knowing one changes nothing about the other:
$$
f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_X(x)\,f_Y(y) \quad\text{for all } x,y.
$$
Two consequences run the rest of statistics. **(1) Independence makes products factor**,
$\mathbb{E}[XY] = \mathbb{E}[X]\,\mathbb{E}[Y]$, which forces $\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=0$
(though zero covariance does *not* run the other way). **(2) Variances of independent
variables add**: $\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\sum_i X_i\right) = \sum_i
\operatorname{Var}(X_i)$. When the $X_i$ are also **identically distributed** — the
"**i.i.d.**" assumption — the sample mean $\bar X_n$ centers on the true mean with variance
$\sigma^2/n$, so its spread shrinks like $1/\sqrt{n}$. That single fact is why more data
helps, and why it helps *exactly that fast*.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The factorization $f_{X,Y} = f_X f_Y$ will look like a
definition we simply *declare*, but by the end you should read it as the only honest way to
write "these two quantities share no information." The variance-adds rule will look like a
happy cancellation, but it is the precise algebraic shadow of the covariances being zero —
and the moment the data are *not* independent, those covariances come back and the
$1/\sqrt{n}$ promise is broken. Holding that in view is what separates the slogan "assume
i.i.d." from understanding what you have actually assumed.

# Why we are here: the assumption hiding under every formula {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **what, exactly, are we promising when we
say a dataset is i.i.d., and what does that promise buy us?** Every derivation below is an
answer to part of that question. If a step ever feels like bookkeeping, return here.

## The problem independence was invented to solve

Probability began, in the seventeenth-century correspondence between Blaise Pascal and
Pierre de Fermat over gambling problems, with a quiet assumption nobody bothered to name:
that one roll of the dice does not remember the last. When they computed the chance of a
run of outcomes by *multiplying* the chances of each, they were using independence —
without a definition for it. The dice have no memory, so the probabilities multiply. That
multiplication, used on faith for a century, is the thing this article makes into a precise,
checkable property of random variables.

The need to *name* it sharpened when probability stopped being about dice and started being
about **data**. By the time Jacob Bernoulli proved his law of large numbers (1713) — the
statement that the average of many trials closes in on the true probability — and Abraham de
Moivre and later Laplace and Gauss developed the normal approximation to sums, every result
rested on the same hypothesis: that the trials were *separate draws from the same mechanism*,
each blind to the others. Bernoulli's trials were independent **and** identically
distributed; that is precisely why their average behaved. Strip either word away and the
theorems fail.

The modern phrasing — "let $X_1,\dots,X_n$ be i.i.d." — is the compressed inheritance of
all of this. It is the first line of nearly every proof in mathematical statistics, the
default model for a random sample, and the silent premise behind the formula
$\text{standard error} = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$ that appears on every polling crosstab and every
A/B-test dashboard. It is also, in real data, **frequently false** — and the gap between
the assumption and the reality is where careful practitioners earn their keep. So we are
going to take the phrase apart word by word.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What does the abbreviation i.i.d. stand for, and which of the two 'i's is doing the
work?"* It stands for **independent and identically distributed**. *Both* words matter, and
they do different jobs:

- **Identically distributed** means every $X_i$ has the *same* distribution — the same pmf
  or pdf, hence the same mean $\mu$ and the same variance $\sigma^2$. This is what lets us
  speak of *the* population mean we are estimating.
- **Independent** means the $X_i$ carry no information about each other — knowing $X_3$
  tells you nothing about $X_7$. This is what makes the variances add and the sample mean
  concentrate.

A common trap answer is to treat them as one idea. They are separable: a time series can be
identically distributed but *not* independent (today's temperature and tomorrow's share a
distribution but are correlated); a dataset can be independent but *not* identically
distributed (heights of independently sampled adults and infants). Both halves are needed
for the clean $\sigma^2/n$ result.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any factorization, hold the picture. Independence is a statement about a *joint
distribution* — the cloud of $(X,Y)$ pairs from [PROB-07]. Two variables are independent
when **slicing the cloud at any value of $X$ leaves the shape of $Y$ unchanged.** Walk
along the $x$-axis from one vertical slice to another; if the conditional spread and center
of $Y$ never budge — same shape, just possibly fewer points — the variables are
independent. If the slices tilt, shift, or change shape as you move, they are dependent.

@fig-slices makes this concrete with two scatterplots that share *identical* marginal
histograms — the same spread of $X$-values and the same spread of $Y$-values — but tell
opposite stories about the joint behaviour.

```{python}
#| label: fig-slices
#| fig-cap: "Independence is about the *joint* shape, not the marginals. **Left:** independent $X$ and $Y$ — the vertical slices (grey bands) all have the same centred, equal-width spread of $Y$; learning $X$ tells you nothing about $Y$. **Right:** dependent $X$ and $Y$ with the *same marginal histograms* (top and right) — yet the slices drift upward as $X$ grows. The marginals cannot see the difference; the joint cloud can."
rng = np.random.default_rng(20)
n = 1200

# Left: independent
x_ind = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
y_ind = rng.normal(0, 1, n)

# Right: dependent but with (approximately) the same marginals as the left.
# Build a correlated pair via a shared latent, then it is not independent.
z = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
x_dep = z
y_dep = 0.8 * z + np.sqrt(1 - 0.8**2) * rng.normal(0, 1, n)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 4.2))
for ax, (xx, yy, ttl) in zip(
    axes,
    [(x_ind, y_ind, "Independent: slices look identical"),
     (x_dep, y_dep, "Dependent: slices drift")],
):
    ax.scatter(xx, yy, s=8, color=POINT, alpha=0.35, zorder=2)
    for c in (-1.5, -0.5, 0.5, 1.5):
        ax.axvspan(c - 0.18, c + 0.18, color=ACCENT, alpha=0.10, zorder=1)
    ax.set_xlim(-3.4, 3.4); ax.set_ylim(-3.4, 3.4)
    ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$y$")
    ax.set_title(ttl, fontsize=10.5)
plt.tight_layout()
```

Notice the punchline already visible in the figure: the *marginal* histograms (the spreads
of $X$ alone and $Y$ alone) are essentially the same on both sides — yet the left is
independent and the right is not. **Independence is invisible to the marginals.** It lives
entirely in how the two fit together, which is exactly why we need the joint distribution of
[PROB-07] to define it, and why a single correlation number (also from [PROB-07]) can miss it.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Intuitively, what is the difference between independence and being uncorrelated?"*
**Uncorrelated** means the *straight-line* association is zero — no linear tilt to the cloud
(@fig-slices, slices don't drift on average). **Independent** is far stronger: it means the
slices are identical in *every* respect — center, spread, skew, shape — not just on average.
Independence implies uncorrelated; the reverse fails because a cloud can have a perfectly
symmetric curve (a $\cap$ or a ring) with no net tilt yet obvious dependence. We prove the
forward implication in @sec-cov-zero and exhibit the famous counterexample to the converse
in @sec-converse-fails.
:::

# Formal development {#sec-formal}

## Defining independence by factorization {#sec-def}

We start from the definition of independence for **events**, established in [PROB-01]: two
events $A$ and $B$ are independent when $P(A\cap B) = P(A)\,P(B)$ — the probability of both
is the product of the probabilities, which is the formal way to say "$B$ happening does not
shift the chance of $A$." Random variables are functions on the sample space ([PROB-02]), so
the natural definition of independence for random variables is: *every* event built from $X$
is independent of *every* event built from $Y$.

::: {#def-independence}
**Definition (independent random variables).** Random variables $X$ and $Y$ are
**independent** if, for every pair of values $x$ and $y$,
$$
P(X \le x,\ Y \le y) = P(X \le x)\,P(Y \le y),
$$ {#eq-indep-cdf}
i.e. the **joint CDF factors** into the product of the marginal CDFs,
$F_{X,Y}(x,y) = F_X(x)\,F_Y(y)$. Equivalently — and this is the form we will use constantly
— the **joint pmf/pdf factors** into the product of the marginals,
$$
\underbrace{p_{X,Y}(x,y)}_{\text{discrete}} = p_X(x)\,p_Y(y),
\qquad
\underbrace{f_{X,Y}(x,y)}_{\text{continuous}} = f_X(x)\,f_Y(y),
\qquad\text{for all } x,y.
$$ {#eq-indep-factor}
:::

Every symbol here was built earlier: $F_{X,Y}$ is the joint CDF, $p_{X,Y}$ and $f_{X,Y}$ the
joint pmf and pdf from [PROB-07], and $F_X, p_X, f_X$ the *marginals* obtained there by
summing or integrating the other variable out. The single new content of @def-independence is
the word **factors**: the two-dimensional object on the left is the product of two
one-dimensional objects on the right.

Why is "factors" the right formalization of "carries no information"? Recall the conditional
density from [PROB-01]/[PROB-09], $f_{Y\mid X}(y\mid x) = f_{X,Y}(x,y)/f_X(x)$ — the
distribution of $Y$ *once you know* $X=x$. Substitute the factored form @eq-indep-factor:
$$
f_{Y\mid X}(y \mid x) = \frac{f_{X,Y}(x,y)}{f_X(x)} = \frac{f_X(x)\,f_Y(y)}{f_X(x)} = f_Y(y).
$$
The conditional distribution of $Y$ given $X=x$ is just $f_Y(y)$ — **it does not depend on
$x$ at all.** Conditioning on $X$ changed nothing. That is precisely the slices-don't-move
picture of @fig-slices, now in symbols: independence is exactly the statement that the
conditional distribution equals the marginal. (We are borrowing the conditional-density
*formula* from [PROB-09] here only to interpret the definition; nothing below depends on it.)

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is checking the factorization at every $(x,y)$ really necessary — can't I just check the
means or the covariance?"* No, and this is the heart of the topic. Independence is a
statement about the **entire** joint distribution: it must hold for *all* $x$ and *all* $y$
simultaneously. Matching means, or even matching covariance, is necessary but nowhere near
sufficient — that is exactly the gap that makes "uncorrelated $\ne$ independent." To
*establish* independence you verify the factorization (or argue it from the data-generating
mechanism: "these were collected by separate, unrelated draws"). To *refute* it, a single
$(x,y)$ where the product fails is enough.
:::

## Mutual independence of many variables {#sec-mutual}

We rarely have just two variables; a dataset has $n$ of them. The definition extends in the
obvious way, but with one subtlety worth stating because interviewers love it.

::: {#def-mutual}
**Definition (mutual independence).** Random variables $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$ are
**mutually independent** if their joint pmf/pdf factors into the product of *all* the
marginals,
$$
f_{X_1,\dots,X_n}(x_1,\dots,x_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} f_{X_i}(x_i)
\qquad\text{for all } x_1,\dots,x_n.
$$ {#eq-mutual}
:::

The subtlety: **mutual independence is strictly stronger than pairwise independence.** It is
possible for every *pair* $(X_i, X_j)$ to be independent while the *whole collection* is not
— the triple can carry joint information that no pair reveals. The classic example: let
$X_1, X_2$ be independent fair coin flips coded as $0/1$, and define $X_3 = X_1 \oplus X_2$
(their XOR — i.e. $X_3 = 1$ exactly when the two flips differ). Then each pair is independent
(knowing one flip tells you nothing about the XOR), yet the three together are deterministic:
$X_3$ is fixed once you know $X_1$ and $X_2$. So $f_{X_1,X_2,X_3} \ne f_{X_1}f_{X_2}f_{X_3}$.
When we write "i.i.d." we always mean **mutual** independence, the full factorization
@eq-mutual — anything weaker would not make the variance computations below go through.

## Independence makes the expectation of a product factor {#sec-product}

The first payoff of factorization is the workhorse identity behind everything that follows.

::: {#thm-product}
**Theorem (product rule for expectation under independence).** If $X$ and $Y$ are
independent and have finite means, then
$$
\mathbb{E}[XY] = \mathbb{E}[X]\,\mathbb{E}[Y].
$$ {#eq-product}
More generally, for independent $X$ and $Y$ and any functions $g,h$,
$\mathbb{E}[g(X)h(Y)] = \mathbb{E}[g(X)]\,\mathbb{E}[h(Y)]$.
:::

::: {.proof}
We prove the continuous case; the discrete case is identical with sums replacing integrals.
Start from the **law of the unconscious statistician** for two variables, established in
[PROB-05]/[PROB-07]: for any function $u(x,y)$, its expectation is the integral against the
joint density,
$$
\mathbb{E}[u(X,Y)] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} u(x,y)\,f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dx\,dy.
$$
Apply this with $u(x,y) = xy$:
$$
\mathbb{E}[XY] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} xy\,f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dx\,dy.
$$
Now use independence, @eq-indep-factor, to replace the joint density by the product
$f_X(x)f_Y(y)$:
$$
\mathbb{E}[XY] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} xy\,f_X(x)\,f_Y(y)\,dx\,dy.
$$
The integrand now *separates* — it is a function of $x$ times a function of $y$. Group the
$x$-pieces and the $y$-pieces:
$$
\mathbb{E}[XY] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}
\bigl[x\,f_X(x)\bigr]\bigl[y\,f_Y(y)\bigr]\,dx\,dy.
$$
The inner integral is over $x$, and the entire $y$-factor $y\,f_Y(y)$ is a *constant* as far
as the $dx$ integral is concerned, so it pulls outside the inner integral:
$$
\mathbb{E}[XY] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y\,f_Y(y)
\left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x\,f_X(x)\,dx\right] dy.
$$
The bracketed inner integral is just $\mathbb{E}[X]$ — it is a number, not a function of
$y$ — so it too pulls outside the remaining integral:
$$
\mathbb{E}[XY] = \mathbb{E}[X]\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y\,f_Y(y)\,dy = \mathbb{E}[X]\,\mathbb{E}[Y],
$$
because the last integral is the definition of $\mathbb{E}[Y]$. The general $g,h$ version is
identical: replace $x$ by $g(x)$ and $y$ by $h(y)$ throughout, and use
$\mathbb{E}[g(X)] = \int g(x)f_X(x)\,dx$ (LOTUS again) at the final step. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The one move that made this work was independence letting us **separate the integrand into
an $x$-part and a $y$-part**. Without factorization, $f_{X,Y}(x,y)$ does not split, the
double integral does not decouple, and $\mathbb{E}[XY]$ is generally *not*
$\mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y]$. Hold onto that — it is the entire reason covariance is about to
vanish.

## Independence forces zero covariance {#sec-cov-zero}

We can now settle the first half of the loose thread [PROB-07] left dangling.

::: {#cor-cov-zero}
**Corollary (independence $\Rightarrow$ uncorrelated).** If $X$ and $Y$ are independent (and
have finite variances), then $\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) = 0$, and hence their correlation is
zero.
:::

::: {.proof}
Recall the **computational form of covariance** derived in [PROB-07]:
$$
\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) = \mathbb{E}[XY] - \mathbb{E}[X]\,\mathbb{E}[Y].
$$
(This itself comes from expanding the definition $\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) =
\mathbb{E}[(X-\mu_X)(Y-\mu_Y)]$ and using linearity of expectation — [PROB-07] does it in
full.) By @thm-product, independence gives $\mathbb{E}[XY] = \mathbb{E}[X]\,\mathbb{E}[Y]$.
Substitute:
$$
\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) = \mathbb{E}[X]\,\mathbb{E}[Y] - \mathbb{E}[X]\,\mathbb{E}[Y] = 0.
$$
Correlation is covariance divided by the (positive) product of standard deviations, so it is
zero whenever the covariance is. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

So independence is a *sufficient* condition for zero covariance. The next section shows it is
not *necessary* — the converse is false, and understanding why is one of the most reliable
ways to separate a fluent answer from a memorized one.

## The converse fails: uncorrelated need not mean independent {#sec-converse-fails}

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Zero covariance does not imply independence.** Covariance measures only the *linear*
co-movement of two variables. A pair can be locked together by a perfectly deterministic
*nonlinear* relationship — so dependent that one determines the other exactly — and still
have covariance zero, because the relationship has no net linear tilt. Treating
"uncorrelated" as "independent" is one of the most common and most punished errors in
applied statistics.
:::

Here is the cleanest counterexample, the one to keep in your pocket. Let $X$ be any
**symmetric** continuous random variable — meaning its density satisfies $f_X(-x) = f_X(x)$
for all $x$ (the graph is a mirror image across the vertical axis) — for instance $X$ uniform
on $[-1,1]$, where $f_X = \tfrac12$ on the whole interval, or $X$ standard normal, whose
density $e^{-x^2/2}/\sqrt{2\pi}$ depends on $x$ only through $x^2$. Define
$$
Y = X^2.
$$
Then $Y$ is *completely determined* by $X$ — they could not be more dependent. Yet their
covariance is zero. To see it, we first establish the one fact the example leans on: **every
odd moment of a symmetric variable is zero.**

Claim: if $f_X(-x)=f_X(x)$, then $\mathbb{E}[X^k] = 0$ for every *odd* power $k$. By LOTUS
([PROB-05]), $\mathbb{E}[X^k] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^k f_X(x)\,dx$. Split the integral at
$0$ into its negative and positive halves:
$$
\mathbb{E}[X^k] = \int_{-\infty}^{0} x^k f_X(x)\,dx + \int_{0}^{\infty} x^k f_X(x)\,dx.
$$
In the first integral substitute $u = -x$ (so $x = -u$, $dx = -du$; as $x$ runs from
$-\infty$ to $0$, $u$ runs from $+\infty$ to $0$). Then $x^k = (-u)^k = -u^k$ because $k$ is
odd, and $f_X(x) = f_X(-u) = f_X(u)$ by symmetry. The flip of the limits absorbs the minus
sign from $dx$:
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{0} x^k f_X(x)\,dx
= \int_{+\infty}^{0} (-u^k)\,f_X(u)\,(-du)
= -\int_{0}^{\infty} u^k f_X(u)\,du.
$$
That is the *negative* of the second integral (just with $u$ as the dummy name instead of
$x$). Adding the two halves, they cancel exactly:
$$
\mathbb{E}[X^k] = -\int_{0}^{\infty} u^k f_X(u)\,du + \int_{0}^{\infty} x^k f_X(x)\,dx = 0.
$$
Taking $k=1$ gives $\mathbb{E}[X] = 0$, and $k=3$ gives $\mathbb{E}[X^3] = 0$ — the two facts
we need. (For a *discrete* symmetric $X$ the same argument runs with $p_X(-x)=p_X(x)$ and the
sum split into negative and positive values; the term at $x$ and the term at $-x$ are
negatives of each other for odd $k$ and cancel pairwise.)

Now compute the covariance directly, using the computational form from [PROB-07] and
$XY = X\cdot X^2 = X^3$:
$$
\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) = \mathbb{E}[XY] - \mathbb{E}[X]\,\mathbb{E}[Y]
= \mathbb{E}[X^3] - \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[X]}_{=\,0}\,\mathbb{E}[Y]
= \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[X^3]}_{=\,0} - 0 = 0,
$$
where both $\mathbb{E}[X^3]=0$ and $\mathbb{E}[X]=0$ are exactly the odd-moment fact just
proved. So $\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=0$ while $Y=X^2$ is a deterministic function of $X$. They
are uncorrelated and maximally dependent at the same time. @fig-converse draws it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-converse
#| fig-cap: "Uncorrelated but utterly dependent. With $X$ uniform on $[-1,1]$ and $Y=X^2$, every point lies *exactly* on the parabola (left) — knowing $X$ pins down $Y$ completely. Yet the cloud has no net linear tilt: the least-squares line (red) is flat, and the sample covariance is essentially zero (right, shown across many samples, scattering around $0$). Covariance is blind to the curve."
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
x = rng.uniform(-1, 1, 400)
y = x**2

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 3.9))

axL.scatter(x, y, s=14, color=POINT, alpha=0.6, zorder=3)
# least-squares line through the cloud, to show it is flat
b1 = np.cov(x, y, bias=True)[0, 1] / np.var(x)
b0 = y.mean() - b1 * x.mean()
xs = np.linspace(-1, 1, 100)
axL.plot(xs, b0 + b1 * xs, color=LINE, label=f"OLS line (slope {b1:.3f} $\\approx 0$)")
axL.set_xlabel("$X$"); axL.set_ylabel("$Y = X^2$")
axL.set_title("Deterministic, yet flat on average")
axL.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

# sampling distribution of the covariance estimate
covs = []
for _ in range(2000):
    xs2 = rng.uniform(-1, 1, 200)
    ys2 = xs2**2
    covs.append(np.cov(xs2, ys2, bias=True)[0, 1])
axR.hist(covs, bins=40, color=FILL, edgecolor="white")
axR.axvline(0, color=LINE, lw=2)
axR.set_xlabel("sample covariance of $(X, X^2)$")
axR.set_ylabel("frequency")
axR.set_title("Centered at zero across 2000 samples")
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"population Cov(X, X^2) = E[X^3] - E[X]E[X^2] = 0 (X symmetric)")
print(f"mean sample covariance over 2000 draws = {np.mean(covs): .4f}")
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Give me a case where two variables are uncorrelated but dependent."* The $Y = X^2$ example
above is the canonical answer — state it in one breath: *"Take $X$ symmetric about zero and
$Y=X^2$; then $\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=\mathbb{E}[X^3]=0$ because every odd moment of a
symmetric variable vanishes (the derivation just above), yet $Y$ is a deterministic function
of $X$."* A second good answer: a point uniformly distributed on the unit circle, written as
$X=\cos\theta,\ Y=\sin\theta$ with the angle $\theta$ uniform on $[0,2\pi)$ (density
$\tfrac{1}{2\pi}$). All three required integrals vanish over a full period: $\mathbb{E}[X] =
\tfrac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}\cos\theta\,d\theta = 0$, likewise $\mathbb{E}[Y]=0$, and using
$\cos\theta\sin\theta = \tfrac12\sin 2\theta$,
$$
\mathbb{E}[XY] = \tfrac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}\cos\theta\,\sin\theta\,d\theta
= \tfrac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}\tfrac12\sin 2\theta\,d\theta
= \tfrac{1}{2\pi}\Bigl[-\tfrac14\cos 2\theta\Bigr]_0^{2\pi} = 0,
$$
so $\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) = \mathbb{E}[XY]-\mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y] = 0$ — yet $X$ and $Y$
are locked together by $X^2+Y^2=1$, so knowing one narrows the other to two values. **The one
exception worth naming:** for the
*jointly normal* distribution (and only there among the common cases), zero covariance
*does* imply independence — which is exactly why people who live in Gaussian-world slip into
conflating the two. That special case is proved in [PROB-17] when the multivariate normal is
built.
:::

# Sums of random variables: where independence pays off {#sec-sums}

The reason independence is *the* assumption of statistics is that data come in sums and
averages — the sample mean, the sample total, the count of successes — and independence is
what makes the spread of those sums computable. We build the two rules now.

## The mean of a sum: linearity needs nothing {#sec-mean-sum}

First, the easy and underappreciated half: **the expectation of a sum is always the sum of
the expectations**, independence or not.

::: {#thm-mean-sum}
**Theorem (expectation of a sum).** For *any* random variables $X_1,\dots,X_n$ with finite
means — independent or not, identically distributed or not —
$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^n \mathbb{E}[X_i].
$$ {#eq-mean-sum}
:::

::: {.proof}
This is precisely the **linearity of expectation** proved in [PROB-05]: for two variables,
$\mathbb{E}[X+Y] = \mathbb{E}[X] + \mathbb{E}[Y]$, with no assumption on how $X$ and $Y$
relate. We extend to $n$ variables by induction. The base case $n=1$ is the bare identity
$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{1} X_i\right] = \mathbb{E}[X_1] = \sum_{i=1}^{1}\mathbb{E}[X_i]$
— a single term on each side, so there is nothing to combine. Assume the
result for $n-1$ variables; then, writing $S_{n-1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} X_i$ and applying
two-variable linearity to $S_{n-1}$ and $X_n$,
$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right]
= \mathbb{E}[S_{n-1} + X_n]
= \mathbb{E}[S_{n-1}] + \mathbb{E}[X_n]
= \sum_{i=1}^{n-1}\mathbb{E}[X_i] + \mathbb{E}[X_n]
= \sum_{i=1}^n \mathbb{E}[X_i],
$$
using the inductive hypothesis in the third step. The chain used linearity only, never
independence. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

It is worth pausing on *why* this needs no independence, because it surprises people. The
expectation of a sum is computed from the **joint** distribution, but it only ever touches
the *marginals*: $\mathbb{E}[X+Y] = \int\!\int (x+y) f_{X,Y}\,dx\,dy$ splits into
$\int\!\int x f_{X,Y} + \int\!\int y f_{X,Y}$, and integrating out the unused variable in
each piece collapses $f_{X,Y}$ to a marginal — *regardless of whether it factored.* Adding
is a linear operation, and linear operations never see the dependence structure. That is the
intuition for why means add unconditionally while — as we are about to see — variances do
not.

## The variance of a sum: the general identity {#sec-var-sum-general}

Now the variance. We first derive the *fully general* formula — no independence assumed —
because it shows exactly where independence will earn its keep.

::: {#thm-var-sum}
**Theorem (variance of a sum, general).** For any random variables $X_1,\dots,X_n$ with
finite variances,
$$
\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right)
= \sum_{i=1}^n \operatorname{Var}(X_i)
\;+\; \sum_{i\ne j} \operatorname{Cov}(X_i, X_j)
= \sum_{i=1}^n \operatorname{Var}(X_i)
\;+\; 2\sum_{i<j} \operatorname{Cov}(X_i, X_j).
$$ {#eq-var-sum-general}
:::

::: {.proof}
Write $S = \sum_{i=1}^n X_i$ and let $\mu_i = \mathbb{E}[X_i]$, so by @thm-mean-sum
$\mathbb{E}[S] = \sum_i \mu_i$ and the centred sum is $S - \mathbb{E}[S] = \sum_i (X_i -
\mu_i)$. By the definition of variance ([PROB-06]),
$$
\operatorname{Var}(S) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\bigl(S - \mathbb{E}[S]\bigr)^2\right]
= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\left(\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mu_i)\right)^{\!2}\,\right].
$$
Expand the square of a sum. The square of $\sum_i a_i$ is the double sum $\sum_i \sum_j a_i
a_j$ over *all* ordered pairs $(i,j)$ — this is just the distributive law,
$(a_1+\dots+a_n)(a_1+\dots+a_n)$ multiplied out. With $a_i = X_i - \mu_i$:
$$
\operatorname{Var}(S) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=1}^n (X_i - \mu_i)(X_j - \mu_j)\right].
$$
Push the expectation through the (finite) double sum by linearity:
$$
\operatorname{Var}(S) = \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=1}^n \mathbb{E}\bigl[(X_i - \mu_i)(X_j - \mu_j)\bigr].
$$
Now split the double sum into the **diagonal** terms ($i=j$) and the **off-diagonal** terms
($i \ne j$). On the diagonal, $\mathbb{E}[(X_i-\mu_i)(X_i-\mu_i)] =
\mathbb{E}[(X_i-\mu_i)^2] = \operatorname{Var}(X_i)$ by definition. Off the diagonal,
$\mathbb{E}[(X_i-\mu_i)(X_j-\mu_j)] = \operatorname{Cov}(X_i, X_j)$ by the definition of
covariance ([PROB-07]). Therefore
$$
\operatorname{Var}(S) = \sum_{i=1}^n \operatorname{Var}(X_i) + \sum_{i \ne j} \operatorname{Cov}(X_i, X_j).
$$
Finally, covariance is symmetric — $\operatorname{Cov}(X_i, X_j) = \operatorname{Cov}(X_j,
X_i)$ — so each unordered pair $\{i,j\}$ appears twice in the off-diagonal sum, once as
$(i,j)$ and once as $(j,i)$. Collecting them gives $2\sum_{i<j}\operatorname{Cov}(X_i,X_j)$,
the second form. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

For two variables, @eq-var-sum-general is the identity $\operatorname{Var}(X+Y) =
\operatorname{Var}(X) + \operatorname{Var}(Y) + 2\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)$ that [PROB-07]
derived — this is its $n$-variable generalization. The crucial structural fact is now in
plain view: **the variance of a sum is the sum of the variances *plus a correction made
entirely of covariances*.** The correction is the price of dependence.

## The variance of a sum under independence: the covariances vanish {#sec-var-sum-indep}

::: {#cor-var-sum-indep}
**Corollary (variances add under independence).** If $X_1,\dots,X_n$ are **(pairwise)
independent** — or merely **uncorrelated** — with finite variances, then
$$
\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^n \operatorname{Var}(X_i).
$$ {#eq-var-sum-indep}
:::

::: {.proof}
By @cor-cov-zero, independence forces $\operatorname{Cov}(X_i, X_j) = 0$ for every $i \ne j$.
(Uncorrelatedness gives the same thing by definition, which is why it is all we actually
need here.) Drop the now-zero covariance correction in @eq-var-sum-general:
$$
\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right)
= \sum_{i=1}^n \operatorname{Var}(X_i) + \underbrace{\sum_{i\ne j}\operatorname{Cov}(X_i,X_j)}_{=\,0}
= \sum_{i=1}^n \operatorname{Var}(X_i). \qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

Two things are worth making explicit, because both are exam-bait.

**First**, the corollary needs only *uncorrelatedness*, not full independence — the proof
used nothing but $\operatorname{Cov}=0$. Independence is the most common *reason* the
covariances are zero, but it is more than the variance rule strictly requires. State this
precisely and you signal you understand the dependency chain rather than a slogan.

**Second**, contrast it sharply with the mean. The mean of a sum **always** adds
(@thm-mean-sum); the variance of a sum adds **only** when the cross-covariances vanish.
Variance is a *quadratic* quantity — it comes from squaring — and squaring a sum produces
cross terms that a linear operation never would. This is the same "$a^2$ versus $a$"
fingerprint of the square that [PROB-06] flagged in $\operatorname{Var}(aX+b) =
a^2\operatorname{Var}(X)$: linearity is blind to dependence, but anything quadratic is not.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Do not assume variances add.** The single most common error in applied variance
calculations is writing $\operatorname{Var}(\sum X_i) = \sum \operatorname{Var}(X_i)$ when
the $X_i$ are correlated. If the variables move together ($\operatorname{Cov} > 0$), the true
variance is *larger* than the sum of variances — risk compounds, as anyone who has watched a
"diversified" portfolio of correlated assets crash in unison can attest. If they move
oppositely ($\operatorname{Cov} < 0$), the true variance is *smaller* — the basis of hedging
and of variance-reduction tricks. The covariance correction in @eq-var-sum-general is not a
nuisance term to ignore; it is often the most important term in the problem.
:::

# The i.i.d. sample and the $1/\sqrt{n}$ law {#sec-iid}

We now assemble the pieces into the object the rest of statistics is built from.

## What "i.i.d." means precisely {#sec-iid-def}

::: {#def-iid}
**Definition (i.i.d. sample).** Random variables $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$ are
**independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.)** if

1. **(identically distributed)** they all have the same distribution — the same CDF $F$,
   hence the same mean $\mu = \mathbb{E}[X_i]$ and same variance $\sigma^2 =
   \operatorname{Var}(X_i)$ for every $i$; and
2. **(independent)** they are mutually independent in the sense of @def-mutual, so the joint
   density factors as $\prod_{i=1}^n f(x_i)$ — *the same* one-variable density $f$ in every
   factor.

We call such a collection a **random sample of size $n$** from the distribution $F$.
:::

This is the formal model of "draw $n$ separate observations from the same population, each
draw blind to the others." The identical-distribution clause is what lets us speak of the
*single* population mean $\mu$ that all $n$ observations share and that we are usually trying
to estimate; the independence clause is what makes the next computation collapse so cleanly.

## The mean and variance of the sample mean {#sec-sample-mean}

The single most-used statistic is the **sample mean**,
$$
\bar X_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i.
$$ {#eq-sample-mean}
We compute its expectation and its variance from the rules just proved. Both calculations
are short, and both are worth doing by hand once so the famous results stop being magic.

::: {#thm-sample-mean}
**Theorem (mean and variance of $\bar X_n$).** For an i.i.d. sample of size $n$ from a
distribution with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$,
$$
\mathbb{E}[\bar X_n] = \mu,
\qquad
\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n},
\qquad
\operatorname{SD}(\bar X_n) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}.
$$ {#eq-sample-mean-moments}
:::

::: {.proof}
**The expectation.** Apply @thm-mean-sum (which needs no independence) and then pull the
constant $1/n$ out of the expectation, which linearity ([PROB-05]) permits:
$$
\mathbb{E}[\bar X_n]
= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right]
= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \mathbb{E}[X_i]
= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \mu
= \frac{1}{n}\cdot n\mu
= \mu.
$$
The third equality uses *identically distributed*: every $\mathbb{E}[X_i]$ equals the same
$\mu$. So the sample mean is **centered on the true mean** — on average it is exactly right.
(In the vocabulary of the Inference track, $\bar X_n$ is an *unbiased* estimator of $\mu$.)

**The variance.** Here independence finally earns its place. First handle the constant
$1/n$. By the scaling rule $\operatorname{Var}(aX) = a^2\operatorname{Var}(X)$ from [PROB-06],
pulling the $1/n$ out of the variance squares it:
$$
\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n)
= \operatorname{Var}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right).
$$
Now use independence via @cor-var-sum-indep to turn the variance of the sum into the sum of
the variances:
$$
\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^n \operatorname{Var}(X_i)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^n \sigma^2
= \frac{1}{n^2}\cdot n\sigma^2
= \frac{\sigma^2}{n}.
$$
The penultimate equality uses *identically distributed* again: every
$\operatorname{Var}(X_i)$ equals the same $\sigma^2$. Finally, the standard deviation is the
square root of the variance ([PROB-06]):
$$
\operatorname{SD}(\bar X_n) = \sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n)} = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma^2}{n}}
= \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}. \qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

Trace exactly which assumption did which job, because this is the most interview-relevant
sentence in the article. **Identically distributed** gave us the single $\mu$ and single
$\sigma^2$ to sum. **Independence** is what let us drop the covariance correction and write
$\operatorname{Var}(\sum X_i) = n\sigma^2$ instead of $n\sigma^2 + (\text{covariances})$.
Take away independence and the $\sigma^2/n$ becomes $\sigma^2/n + (\text{correlation
term})$, and — as we will see in @sec-edge — that term can dwarf the $\sigma^2/n$ and make a
huge sample worth almost nothing.

## What $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ means, and why it is the engine of statistics {#sec-root-n}

Read @eq-sample-mean-moments as a statement about *information*. The variance of the sample
mean is $\sigma^2/n$: it **shrinks toward zero as $n$ grows**. So as you collect more data,
the sample mean clusters ever more tightly around the truth $\mu$. This is the precise,
quantitative reason that "more data helps" — and the formula tells you it helps at an exact
rate.

That rate is the subtle part. The *standard deviation* of $\bar X_n$ — the spread in the
original units — is $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$, not $\sigma/n$. To **halve** the standard error of
your estimate you must **quadruple** the sample size. Diminishing returns are baked into the
square root: the first hundred observations buy you a lot of precision; going from ten
thousand to ten thousand and one buys almost nothing. @fig-root-n shows both the shrinking
spread and the brutal $1/\sqrt{n}$ curve.

```{python}
#| label: fig-root-n
#| fig-cap: 'The $1/\sqrt{n}$ law in action. **Left:** the sampling distribution of $\bar X_n$ for an exponential population ($\mu=1,\ \sigma=1$) tightens around the true mean (dashed) as $n$ grows from 5 to 500 — its spread is $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$. **Right:** the standard error $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ (red) against $n$, with the simulated standard deviations of $\bar X_n$ (dots) landing exactly on it. The curve is steep early and nearly flat late: quadrupling $n$ only halves the error.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
mu, sigma = 1.0, 1.0      # Exp(1): mean 1, sd 1
ns = [5, 20, 100, 500]
reps = 6000

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 3.9))

colors = plt.cm.viridis(np.linspace(0.15, 0.85, len(ns)))
for nval, col in zip(ns, colors):
    means = rng.exponential(scale=1.0, size=(reps, nval)).mean(axis=1)
    axL.hist(means, bins=60, density=True, histtype="step",
             color=col, lw=2, label=f"$n={nval}$")
axL.axvline(mu, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.5, label=r"true mean $\mu=1$")
axL.set_xlim(0, 2.2)
axL.set_xlabel(r"$\bar X_n$"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.set_title("Sampling distribution tightens with $n$")
axL.legend(fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)

n_grid = np.arange(2, 501)
se = sigma / np.sqrt(n_grid)
axR.plot(n_grid, se, color=LINE, label=r"$\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ (theory)")
emp_n = [5, 20, 50, 100, 200, 400, 500]
emp_sd = []
for nval in emp_n:
    means = rng.exponential(scale=1.0, size=(8000, nval)).mean(axis=1)
    emp_sd.append(means.std())
axR.scatter(emp_n, emp_sd, color=POINT, s=45, zorder=3, label="simulated SD of $\\bar X_n$")
axR.set_xlabel("sample size $n$"); axR.set_ylabel(r"standard error of $\bar X_n$")
axR.set_title(r"Standard error follows $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$")
axR.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The figure also previews a second miracle this article deliberately does *not* prove: the
left histograms are not just tightening, they are turning into the **bell curve** even
though the underlying population is a lopsided exponential. That is the **Central Limit
Theorem**, and per the track's ownership decisions it belongs to the Statistical Inference
track (its proof needs the convergence machinery of [PROB-20] and [PROB-21]). What *this*
article owns is the prerequisite: the i.i.d. structure and the exact $\sigma^2/n$ variance
that the CLT then describes the *shape* of. We supply the variance; Inference supplies the
limiting law.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"If I double my sample, how much more precise is my estimate of the mean?"* Not twice as
precise. The **variance** of $\bar X_n$ halves (variance is $\sigma^2/n$), but the **standard
error** — the thing reported as $\pm$ on an estimate — only drops by a factor of
$\sqrt 2 \approx 1.41$, because the standard error is $\sigma/\sqrt n$. To cut the standard
error in *half* you need *four times* the data. This $\sqrt n$ scaling is why surveys hit a
wall of diminishing returns and why "just collect more data" is a real but expensive lever.
A sharp follow-up: *"and what if the observations are positively correlated?"* — then the
covariance term in @eq-var-sum-general is positive, the effective sample size is *smaller*
than $n$, and the standard error shrinks even more slowly than $\sigma/\sqrt n$. We make that
quantitative in @sec-edge.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We run the machinery on honest, real-flavored problems — including one at the edge where the
i.i.d. assumption breaks and the formulas mislead.

## Example 1 — combining two independent measurements {#sec-ex-measure}

A laboratory weighs a sample on two independent scales. Scale A has measurement noise with
standard deviation $\sigma_A = 0.40$ g; scale B is older, with $\sigma_B = 0.70$ g. Each
scale reports the true weight $w$ plus independent zero-mean noise, so the two readings are
$X_A = w + \varepsilon_A$ and $X_B = w + \varepsilon_B$ with $\varepsilon_A, \varepsilon_B$
independent. How should we combine them, and how much does combining help?

The naive combination is the simple average $\tfrac12(X_A + X_B)$. Both readings are
unbiased ($\mathbb{E}[X_A] = \mathbb{E}[X_B] = w$), so by @thm-mean-sum the average is
unbiased too. Its variance, using $\operatorname{Var}(aX) = a^2\operatorname{Var}(X)$
([PROB-06]) and then independence (@cor-var-sum-indep) to add the variances:
$$
\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\tfrac12 X_A + \tfrac12 X_B\right)
= \tfrac14\operatorname{Var}(X_A) + \tfrac14\operatorname{Var}(X_B)
= \tfrac14(\sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2).
$$
But the simple average is *not* the best linear combination, because it trusts the noisy
scale as much as the precise one. Consider the general weighted average $\hat w = \alpha X_A
+ (1-\alpha) X_B$ (weights summing to one keep it unbiased). Its variance, again by scaling
and independence, is
$$
\operatorname{Var}(\hat w) = \alpha^2 \sigma_A^2 + (1-\alpha)^2 \sigma_B^2,
$$
a function of the single knob $\alpha$. Minimize it the Calc-1 way — differentiate and set to
zero:
$$
\frac{d}{d\alpha}\Bigl[\alpha^2\sigma_A^2 + (1-\alpha)^2\sigma_B^2\Bigr]
= 2\alpha\sigma_A^2 - 2(1-\alpha)\sigma_B^2 = 0
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\alpha\sigma_A^2 = (1-\alpha)\sigma_B^2.
$$
Solving for $\alpha$:
$$
\alpha\sigma_A^2 + \alpha\sigma_B^2 = \sigma_B^2
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\alpha = \frac{\sigma_B^2}{\sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2}.
$$
The optimal weight on a scale is proportional to the *other* scale's variance — equivalently,
to its **own inverse variance** $1/\sigma_A^2$. This is **inverse-variance weighting**: trust
each measurement in proportion to its precision. @fig-measure plots the variance against
$\alpha$ and marks both the optimum and the inferior simple average.

```{python}
#| label: fig-measure
#| fig-cap: 'Combining two independent measurements with noise SDs $0.40$ g and $0.70$ g. The variance of the weighted estimate $\alpha X_A + (1-\alpha)X_B$ (red curve) is a parabola in the weight $\alpha$. The simple average $\alpha=0.5$ (grey) is beaten by the inverse-variance optimum (green), which leans on the more precise scale A. Independence is what let the two variances simply add to form this parabola.'
sA, sB = 0.40, 0.70
alpha = np.linspace(0, 1, 200)
var = alpha**2 * sA**2 + (1 - alpha)**2 * sB**2
a_star = sB**2 / (sA**2 + sB**2)
var_star = a_star**2 * sA**2 + (1 - a_star)**2 * sB**2
var_simple = 0.25 * (sA**2 + sB**2)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(alpha, var, color=LINE, label=r"$\operatorname{Var}(\hat w)=\alpha^2\sigma_A^2+(1-\alpha)^2\sigma_B^2$")
ax.scatter([a_star], [var_star], color=ACCENT, s=90, zorder=4, edgecolor="white",
           label=fr"optimum $\alpha^*={a_star:.2f}$, var $={var_star:.4f}$")
ax.scatter([0.5], [var_simple], color=GREY, s=70, zorder=4,
           label=fr"simple average, var $={var_simple:.4f}$")
ax.set_xlabel(r"weight $\alpha$ on scale A")
ax.set_ylabel(r"variance of combined estimate")
ax.set_title("Inverse-variance weighting beats the simple average")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"optimal alpha* = sigma_B^2/(sigma_A^2+sigma_B^2) = {a_star:.4f}")
print(f"variance at optimum       = {var_star:.5f}  (SD = {np.sqrt(var_star):.4f} g)")
print(f"variance at simple average= {var_simple:.5f}  (SD = {np.sqrt(var_simple):.4f} g)")
print(f"variance of scale A alone = {sA**2:.5f}  (SD = {sA:.4f} g)")
```

The payoff: combining two independent readings — even one mediocre one — beats either reading
alone, and weighting by precision beats weighting them equally. This is exactly the logic
behind meta-analysis (pooling independent studies), sensor fusion, and the Kalman filter.
Every step relied on independence to add the variances; if the two scales shared a systematic
error (a miscalibrated reference weight), $\operatorname{Cov}(\varepsilon_A,\varepsilon_B) >
0$ and the combination would help far less — the boundary we hit head-on in @sec-edge.

## Example 2 — the standard error of a poll {#sec-ex-poll}

A pollster samples $n$ voters independently and records $X_i = 1$ if voter $i$ supports a
measure, $0$ otherwise. Each $X_i$ is a Bernoulli variable: it equals $1$ with the true
population support $p$ and $0$ with probability $1-p$. (The Bernoulli is catalogued in
[PROB-11]; here we need only its mean and variance, which we derive inline.) For a single
$X_i$,
$$
\mathbb{E}[X_i] = 1\cdot p + 0\cdot(1-p) = p,
$$
$$
\operatorname{Var}(X_i) = \mathbb{E}[X_i^2] - (\mathbb{E}[X_i])^2 = p - p^2 = p(1-p),
$$
where $\mathbb{E}[X_i^2] = 1^2\cdot p + 0^2\cdot(1-p) = p$ because $X_i^2 = X_i$ for a $0/1$
variable. The poll's estimate of support is the sample mean $\hat p = \bar X_n =
\tfrac1n\sum X_i$, which is just the fraction of "yes" responses. By @thm-sample-mean, with
$\mu = p$ and $\sigma^2 = p(1-p)$,
$$
\mathbb{E}[\hat p] = p,
\qquad
\operatorname{Var}(\hat p) = \frac{p(1-p)}{n},
\qquad
\operatorname{SE}(\hat p) = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}.
$$
That last expression is the **standard error of a proportion** — the $\pm$ on every poll. Two
honest readings of it. First, the worst-case spread is at $p = 0.5$, where $p(1-p) = 0.25$ is
largest; this is why "margin of error" tables are computed at $50\%$ — the conservative case.
At $p=0.5$ and $n=1000$, $\operatorname{SE} = \sqrt{0.25/1000} \approx 0.0158$, and the
familiar "$\pm 3$ points" margin is roughly $2\,\operatorname{SE} \approx 0.032$. Second, the
$\sqrt n$ wall: @fig-poll shows the margin of error against $n$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-poll
#| fig-cap: 'The standard error of a poll at the conservative $p=0.5$. **Left:** margin of error ($\approx 2\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}$) collapses fast for small samples, then crawls — going from 1000 to 4000 respondents only halves it (from $\pm 3.2$ to $\pm 1.6$ points). **Right:** simulated $\hat p$ for $n=1000$ over 5000 polls, centred at the truth $p=0.5$ with SD matching $\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}$ (dashed band at $\pm 2$ SE). The $1/\sqrt n$ law is why a national poll of a few thousand suffices, and why ten times the budget barely sharpens it.'
p = 0.5
n_grid = np.arange(100, 4001, 10)
moe = 2 * np.sqrt(p * (1 - p) / n_grid) * 100  # in percentage points

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 3.9))
axL.plot(n_grid, moe, color=LINE)
for nn in (1000, 4000):
    m = 2 * np.sqrt(p*(1-p)/nn) * 100
    axL.scatter([nn], [m], color=ACCENT, s=60, zorder=4, edgecolor="white")
    axL.annotate(f"$n={nn}$\n$\\pm{m:.1f}$ pts", (nn, m),
                 textcoords="offset points", xytext=(8, 10), fontsize=9)
axL.set_xlabel("sample size $n$"); axL.set_ylabel("margin of error (pct points)")
axL.set_title(r"Margin of error $\approx 2\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}$")

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
nn = 1000
phats = rng.binomial(nn, p, size=5000) / nn
se = np.sqrt(p*(1-p)/nn)
axR.hist(phats, bins=45, density=True, color=FILL, edgecolor="white")
axR.axvline(p, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.5, label=r"truth $p=0.5$")
axR.axvspan(p - 2*se, p + 2*se, color=ACCENT, alpha=0.15, label=r"$\pm 2\,$SE")
axR.set_xlabel(r"$\hat p$ (poll estimate)"); axR.set_ylabel("density")
axR.set_title(f"Sampling distribution of $\\hat p$, $n={nn}$")
axR.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"SE at p=0.5, n=1000: {np.sqrt(p*(1-p)/1000):.4f}  -> MOE ~ {2*np.sqrt(p*(1-p)/1000)*100:.2f} pts")
print(f"simulated SD of phat: {phats.std():.4f}  (theory {se:.4f})")
```

Notice this whole calculation is just @thm-sample-mean with a Bernoulli population, and it
silently assumes the voters were sampled **independently and identically** — the same
population $p$, each draw blind to the others. That assumption is exactly what the next
example violates.

## Example 3 (edge of applicability) — when "i.i.d." is a lie {#sec-edge}

Here is the honest boundary every applied track eventually slams into: **real data is often
not i.i.d., and pretending it is makes you overconfident — sometimes catastrophically.** We
make the failure quantitative.

Suppose we are estimating a daily mean — say, average page-load latency — from $n$ consecutive
days. The identical-distribution part might be fine (no trend over the window), but
consecutive days are **correlated**: a slow server today tends to be slow tomorrow. Model the
observations as having common variance $\sigma^2$ and a constant pairwise correlation $\rho$
between *every* pair, $\operatorname{Cov}(X_i, X_j) = \rho\sigma^2$ for $i\ne j$ (this is the
"equicorrelation" model; real autocorrelation decays with distance, but constant $\rho$ makes
the arithmetic clean and the lesson identical). Now compute the *true* variance of the sample
mean using the **general** formula @eq-var-sum-general, which keeps the covariances:
$$
\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\left[\sum_{i=1}^n \sigma^2 + \sum_{i\ne j}\rho\sigma^2\right].
$$
There are $n$ diagonal terms and $n(n-1)$ off-diagonal ordered pairs, so
$$
\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\Bigl[n\sigma^2 + n(n-1)\rho\sigma^2\Bigr]
= \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\Bigl[1 + (n-1)\rho\Bigr].
$$ {#eq-corr-var}
Read @eq-corr-var against the i.i.d. answer $\sigma^2/n$. The correction factor is $1 +
(n-1)\rho$. When $\rho = 0$ it is $1$ and we recover @thm-sample-mean. But when $\rho > 0$,
the factor *grows with $n$* — and for large $n$ the variance behaves like $\sigma^2\rho$,
which **does not go to zero at all.** No matter how many correlated days you collect, your
estimate of the mean stops improving. The naive standard error $\sigma/\sqrt n$ understates
the true uncertainty by a factor of $\sqrt{1+(n-1)\rho}$, and you build confidence intervals
that are far too narrow. @fig-edge shows the gap blowing open.

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge
#| fig-cap: 'The edge of applicability: i.i.d. assumed, dependence real. With equicorrelation $\rho$ between observations, the *true* variance of $\bar X_n$ (solid) is $\frac{\sigma^2}{n}[1+(n-1)\rho]$, while the naive i.i.d. formula $\sigma^2/n$ (dashed) keeps shrinking. **Left:** for $\rho=0.3$ the true variance flattens out near $\sigma^2\rho$ — more data buys almost nothing past a point. **Right:** simulated $\bar X_n$ for $n=200$ correlated observations is far more spread out than the i.i.d. theory (red band) predicts; reporting $\sigma/\sqrt n$ here would badly overstate one''s certainty.'
sigma2 = 1.0
n_grid = np.arange(2, 401)
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 3.9))

for rho, col in zip([0.0, 0.1, 0.3], [ACCENT, "#b8860b", LINE]):
    true_var = sigma2 / n_grid * (1 + (n_grid - 1) * rho)
    axL.plot(n_grid, true_var, color=col, label=fr"true, $\rho={rho}$")
axL.plot(n_grid, sigma2 / n_grid, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.6, label=r"naive i.i.d. $\sigma^2/n$")
axL.set_xlabel("sample size $n$"); axL.set_ylabel(r"$\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n)$")
axL.set_ylim(0, 0.35)
axL.set_title("Correlation defeats the $1/n$ shrink")
axL.legend(fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)

# Simulate equicorrelated data: X_i = sqrt(rho)*Z + sqrt(1-rho)*eps_i (shared latent Z)
rng = np.random.default_rng(99)
rho, nn, reps = 0.3, 200, 5000
Z = rng.normal(0, 1, reps)[:, None]
eps = rng.normal(0, 1, (reps, nn))
X = np.sqrt(rho) * Z + np.sqrt(1 - rho) * eps   # Var=1, pairwise corr=rho
means = X.mean(axis=1)
se_naive = np.sqrt(sigma2 / nn)
se_true = np.sqrt(sigma2 / nn * (1 + (nn - 1) * rho))
axR.hist(means, bins=50, density=True, color=FILL, edgecolor="white")
axR.axvspan(-2*se_naive, 2*se_naive, color=LINE, alpha=0.18,
            label=fr"naive $\pm2\sigma/\sqrt n={2*se_naive:.3f}$")
axR.axvline(0, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.3)
axR.set_xlabel(r"$\bar X_n$ ($n=200$, $\rho=0.3$)"); axR.set_ylabel("density")
axR.set_title("Reality (histogram) far wider than i.i.d. theory")
axR.legend(fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"naive i.i.d. SD of mean : {se_naive:.4f}")
print(f"true SD (eq. 9) of mean : {se_true:.4f}")
print(f"simulated SD of mean    : {means.std():.4f}")
print(f"understatement factor   : {se_true/se_naive:.2f}x")
```

The simulation makes it visceral: with $\rho = 0.3$ and $n = 200$, the true standard error is
roughly $`{python} f"{np.sqrt(1*(1+(200-1)*0.3)/200):.2f}"`$ versus the naive
$`{python} f"{np.sqrt(1/200):.2f}"`$ — an understatement of about
$`{python} f"{np.sqrt((1+(200-1)*0.3)):.1f}"`$-fold. Build a 95% confidence interval with the
naive formula and it will miss the truth far more than 5% of the time.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Give me a real-world example where the i.i.d. assumption fails, and say which half breaks."*
Have several ready and name the failing half each time:

- **Time series** (stock returns, server metrics, weather): **independence** fails —
  consecutive observations are autocorrelated. Identical distribution may also fail if there
  is a trend or regime change (then it is *non-stationary*).
- **Clustered/grouped data** (students within classrooms, patients within hospitals,
  repeated measurements on the same person): **independence** fails within clusters; the
  *effective* sample size is closer to the number of clusters than the number of rows. This
  is why "we have a million rows" can be statistically weak if they come from a hundred
  users.
- **Spatial data** (soil samples, disease incidence by region): **independence** fails —
  nearby locations resemble each other.
- **Survivorship / selection** (only funds that survived appear in the dataset): **identical
  distribution** fails — the sample is not from the population you think.

**What you reach for instead:** for time series, models that build the dependence in (AR/ARMA,
GARCH) or robust standard errors (Newey–West / HAC); for clustered data, mixed-effects
models or cluster-robust standard errors; for spatial data, spatial-correlation models. The
common theme — voice this and you sound senior — is *"i.i.d. is the assumption that lets
variances simply add; when it fails, you must put the covariances back, either by modeling
them or by inflating your standard errors to account for them."* That sentence is the entire
article in one line.
:::

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

Collecting the boundaries, so the applicability call is clean.

- **Dependence (the big one).** The $1/\sqrt n$ shrinkage and the variances-add rule both
  require the cross-covariances to vanish. Positive dependence inflates the true variance
  (@sec-edge) and makes naive standard errors overconfident; negative dependence deflates it
  (the basis of antithetic-variate variance reduction and of hedging). **Never report
  $\sigma/\sqrt n$ without asking whether the observations were really independent.**

- **Not identically distributed.** If the $X_i$ have different means or variances, "the"
  population mean $\mu$ is not well defined and @thm-sample-mean does not apply as stated.
  The variances still add under independence (@cor-var-sum-indep with distinct
  $\operatorname{Var}(X_i)$), but the clean $\sigma^2/n$ collapse needs the common $\sigma^2$.
  Heterogeneous data calls for weighting (Example 1's inverse-variance idea), stratification,
  or an explicit model of how the distribution changes.

- **Uncorrelated mistaken for independent.** The variance rule @cor-var-sum-indep needs only
  zero covariance, so uncorrelatedness is *enough for variance arithmetic*. But many
  arguments downstream — the factorization of a likelihood into a product, the independence
  of a numerator and denominator in [PROB-15]'s $t$ and $F$ constructions — genuinely require
  full independence, and there uncorrelated is **not** enough (@sec-converse-fails). Know
  which one your argument actually uses.

- **Heavy tails / no finite variance.** Every variance result here silently assumes
  $\sigma^2 < \infty$. For heavy-tailed populations (the Cauchy distribution, some financial
  returns) the variance is infinite and $\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n) = \sigma^2/n$ is
  meaningless — the sample mean does *not* concentrate. [PROB-05] flagged this boundary when it
  showed expectations can fail to exist; it bites again here.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is the i.i.d. model the right one, and when is it the wrong one?"* The honest summary
to recite:

- **Use it** when observations are genuinely *separate draws from one stable mechanism*:
  independently sampled survey respondents, repeated independent trials of an experiment,
  rows of a cross-section with no shared structure. In that regime the machinery is exact and
  powerful — unbiased sample means, variances that add, the $\sigma^2/n$ law, and (via
  Inference) the CLT.
- **Do not use it** when there is *memory* (time series), *grouping* (clusters, repeated
  measures), *geography* (spatial), *selection* (survivorship), or *drift* (non-stationarity).
  Model the dependence or inflate the uncertainty; do not assume it away.
- **The deepest caveat:** i.i.d. is an assumption about how the data were *generated*, and no
  amount of staring at the numbers can fully confirm it — it is a claim about the world and
  the sampling process. The most expensive statistical mistakes are made by people who
  treated dependent data as i.i.d. because the formula was convenient.
:::

# How you would actually check and use this {#sec-implementation}

The math is primary; this is the secondary, practical note. In practice you rarely verify the
full factorization @eq-indep-factor directly — it is a statement about an entire joint
distribution. Instead:

- **Argue independence from the data-generating process**, not from the numbers. "These were
  separate respondents contacted at random" is a stronger warrant than any test. Independence
  is a design property first.
- **Look for dependence you can see.** A scatter of consecutive observations (a *lag plot*),
  an autocorrelation function, or residuals plotted in collection order will expose
  time-dependence; grouping variables (user ID, site, region) flag clustering. A flat,
  patternless lag plot is consistent with independence; structure in it refutes it.
- **When dependence is present, put the covariances back.** Either model them (AR/ARMA, mixed
  models) or use a robust standard-error estimator (cluster-robust, HAC/Newey–West) that
  estimates the covariance correction in @eq-var-sum-general rather than assuming it away.
  Libraries (`statsmodels`, `scipy.stats`, the survey packages) expose these directly; what
  they are computing is exactly the inflated variance of @sec-edge.

The single operational habit: **before you divide by $\sqrt n$, ask what made the
observations independent.** If you cannot answer, your standard error is a guess.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[PROB-07 *Joint Distributions, Covariance & Correlation*]**, which built
the joint distribution and the covariance and left the "zero covariance $\ne$ independence"
thread hanging. This article tied it off: independence is factorization, it forces zero
covariance, the converse fails, and — applied to a sample — it makes variances add and the
sample mean concentrate at rate $1/\sqrt n$. From here:

- **[PROB-09 *Conditional Distributions & Conditional Expectation*]** — the sibling of this
  article. Independence is the case where conditioning on $X$ changes nothing; PROB-09 handles
  the general case where it changes everything, building $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ as the best
  predictor.
- **[PROB-11 *Bernoulli, Binomial & Negative-Binomial*]** — the binomial *is* a sum of
  i.i.d. Bernoullis; its mean $np$ and variance $np(1-p)$ are @thm-mean-sum and
  @cor-var-sum-indep applied to Example 2's setup. The catalog (PROB-11–PROB-16) leans on the
  sum rules derived here.
- **[PROB-19 *Chebyshev's Inequality*]** — turns the $\sigma^2/n$ variance into a probability
  bound, delivering the *weak law of large numbers*: the sample mean converges in probability
  to $\mu$. The i.i.d. structure built here is exactly what Chebyshev needs.
- **[PROB-21 *Modes of Convergence*]** and **[PROB-22 *The Empirical CDF*]** — limit theorems
  are theorems about i.i.d. sums; the empirical CDF (and the bootstrap that resamples it) is
  built from an i.i.d. sample. The CLT itself — the bell shape glimpsed in @fig-root-n — lives
  in the **Statistical Inference** track (A2), which by design owns the limit theorems while
  this track owns their raw material.

**Cross-track:** the i.i.d. assumption opens Statistical Inference (A1), and is the standing
hypothesis of Regression (the errors are i.i.d.) and Classification (training examples are
i.i.d. draws). Every one of those tracks links *here* for what the phrase means.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The historical seed.** Jacob Bernoulli's *Ars Conjectandi* (1713) contains the first law
  of large numbers — the original demonstration that an average of independent identical
  trials closes in on the truth, the result that made "i.i.d." worth naming.
- **The dependence that i.i.d. ignores.** For how correlation re-enters and is tamed: the
  theory of stationary processes and the autocovariance function (time-series texts, e.g.
  Brockwell & Davis), and the *effective sample size* $n/(1+(n-1)\rho)$ that @eq-corr-var
  implicitly defines — the number of *independent* observations your correlated sample is
  really worth.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *Why does the sample mean of an
  i.i.d. sample become bell-shaped?* — the Central Limit Theorem, owned by Statistical
  Inference A2, built on [PROB-20]/[PROB-21]. *When does zero covariance actually imply
  independence?* — for the jointly normal distribution, proved in [PROB-17]. *What replaces
  the variance-adds rule for vectors of random variables?* — the covariance-matrix identity
  $\operatorname{Cov}(AX) = A\Sigma A^\top$, built in [PROB-17]–[PROB-18], of which
  @cor-var-sum-indep is the diagonal special case.
